Docker​
Upgrading the Docker version of LangSmith is a bit more involved than the Helm version and may require a small amount of downtime. Please follow the instructions below to upgrade your Docker version of LangSmith.

Update your docker-compose.yml file to the file used in the latest release. You can find this in the LangSmith SDK GitHub repository
Update your .env file with any new environment variables that are required in the new version. These will be detailed in the release notes for the new version.
Run the following command to stop your current LangSmith instance:

docker-compose down

Run the following command to start your new LangSmith instance in the background:

docker-compose up -d
If everything ran successfully, you should see all the LangSmith containers running and healthy.

documents.page_content:
```output
* [SIM=1.726390] The stock market is down 500 points today due to fears of a recession. [{'source': 'news'}]
```

#### Search by vector​

You can also search by vector:

```python
results = vector_store.similarity_search_by_vector(
    embedding=embeddings.embed_query("I love green eggs and ham!"), k=1
)
for doc in results:
    print(f"* {doc.page_content} [{doc.metadata}]")
```

```output
* I had chocalate chip pancakes and fried eggs for breakfast this morning. [{'source': 'tweet'}]
```

#### Other search methods​

There are a variety of other search methods that are not covered in this notebook, such as MMR search or searching by vector. For a full list of the search abilities available for `AstraDBVectorStore` check out the [API reference](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/astradb/vectorstores/langchain_astradb.vectorstores.AstraDBVectorStore.html).

### Query by turning into retriever​

You can also transform the vector store into a retriever for easier usage in your chains. For more information on the different search types and kwargs you can pass, please visit the API reference [here](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/chroma/vectorstores/langchain_chroma.vectorstores.Chroma.html#langchain_chroma.vectorstores.Chroma.as_retriever).

```python
retriever = vector_store.as_retriever(
    search_type="mmr", search_kwargs={"k": 1, "fetch_k": 5}
)
retriever.invoke("Stealing from the bank is a crime", filter={"source": "news"})
```

```output
[Document(metadata={'source': 'news'}, page_content='Robbers broke into the city bank and stole $1 million in cash.')]
```

## Usage for retrieval-augmented generation​

For guides on how to use this vector store for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), see the following sections:

- [Tutorials](/docs/tutorials/)

- [How-to: Question and answer with RAG](https://python.langchain.com/docs/how_to/#qa-with-rag)

- [Retrieval conceptual docs](https://python.langchain.com/docs/concepts/retrieval)

## API reference​

For detailed documentation of all `Chroma` vector store features and configurations head to the API reference: [https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/chroma/vectorstores/langchain_chroma.vectorstores.Chroma.html](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/chroma/vectorstores/langchain_chroma.vectorstores.Chroma.html)

## Related​

- Vector store [conceptual guide](/docs/concepts/vectorstores/)

- Vector store [how-to guides](/docs/how_to/#vector-stores)

metadata.title: How to Deploy to LangGraph Cloud | 🦜🕸️LangGraph

documents.page_content:
Create New Revision¶
When creating a new deployment, a new revision is created by default. Subsequent revisions can be created to deploy new code changes.
Starting from the LangSmith UI...

In the left-hand navigation panel, select LangGraph Platform. The LangGraph Platform view contains a list of existing LangGraph Cloud deployments.
Select an existing deployment to create a new revision for.
In the Deployment view, in the top-right corner, select + New Revision.
In the New Revision modal, fill out the required fields.
Specify the full path to the LangGraph API config file including the file name. For example, if the file langgraph.json is in the root of the repository, simply specify langgraph.json.
Determine if the deployment should be Shareable through LangGraph Studio.
If unchecked, the deployment will only be accessible with a valid LangSmith API key for the workspace.
If checked, the deployment will be accessible through LangGraph Studio to any LangSmith user. A direct URL to LangGraph Studio for the deployment will be provided to share with other LangSmith users.

Specify Environment Variables and secrets. Existing secrets and environment variables are prepopulated. See the Environment Variables reference to configure additional variables for the revision.
Add new secrets or environment variables.
Remove existing secrets or environment variables.
Update the value of existing secrets or environment variables.

Select Submit. After a few seconds, the New Revision modal will close and the new revision will be queued for deployment.

View Build and Server Logs¶
Build and server logs are available for each revision.
Starting from the LangGraph Platform view...

Select the desired revision from the Revisions table. A panel slides open from the right-hand side and the Build tab is selected by default, which displays build logs for the revision.
In the panel, select the Server tab to view server logs for the revision. Server logs are only available after a revision has been deployed.
Within the Server tab, adjust the date/time range picker as needed. By default, the date/time range picker is set to the Last 7 days.

Interrupt Revision¶
Interrupting a revision will stop deployment of the revision.

Undefined Behavior
Interrupted revisions have undefined behavior. This is only useful if you need to deploy a new revision and you already have a revision "stuck" in progress. In the future, this feature may be removed.

Starting from the LangGraph Platform view...

Select the menu icon (three dots) on the right-hand side of the row for the desired revision from the Revisions table.
Select Interrupt from the menu.
A modal will appear. Review the confirmation message. Select Interrupt revision.

Delete Deployment¶
Starting from the LangSmith UI...

In the left-hand navigation panel, select LangGraph Platform. The LangGraph Platform view contains a list of existing LangGraph Cloud deployments.
Select the menu icon (three dots) on the right-hand side of the row for the desired deployment and select Delete.
A Confirmation modal will appear. Select Delete.

Deployment Settings¶
Starting from the LangGraph Platform view...

In the top-right corner, select the gear icon (Deployment Settings).
Update the Git Branch to the desired branch.
Check/uncheck checkbox to Automatically update deployment on push to branch.
Branch creation/deletion and tag creation/deletion events will not trigger an update. Only pushes to an existing branch will trigger an update.
Pushes in quick succession to a branch will not trigger subsequent updates. In the future, this functionality may be changed/improved.

Comments

metadata.description: This tutorial explains how to set up the project locally and submit your initial code contribution.
metadata.title: Setup | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
### Code Formatting​

Formatting for this project is done via [ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/).

To run formatting for docs, cookbook and templates:

```bash
make format
```

To run formatting for a library, run the same command from the relevant library directory:

```bash
cd libs/{LIBRARY}
make format
```

Additionally, you can run the formatter only on the files that have been modified in your current branch as compared to the master branch using the format_diff command:

```bash
make format_diff
```

This is especially useful when you have made changes to a subset of the project and want to ensure your changes are properly formatted without affecting the rest of the codebase.

#### Linting​

Linting for this project is done via a combination of [ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/) and [mypy](http://mypy-lang.org/).

To run linting for docs, cookbook and templates:

```bash
make lint
```

To run linting for a library, run the same command from the relevant library directory:

```bash
cd libs/{LIBRARY}
make lint
```

In addition, you can run the linter only on the files that have been modified in your current branch as compared to the master branch using the lint_diff command:

```bash
make lint_diff
```

This can be very helpful when you've made changes to only certain parts of the project and want to ensure your changes meet the linting standards without having to check the entire codebase.

We recognize linting can be annoying - if you do not want to do it, please contact a project maintainer, and they can help you with it. We do not want this to be a blocker for good code getting contributed.

### Spellcheck​

Spellchecking for this project is done via [codespell](https://github.com/codespell-project/codespell).
Note that `codespell` finds common typos, so it could have false-positive (correctly spelled but rarely used) and false-negatives (not finding misspelled) words.

To check spelling for this project:

```bash
make spell_check
```

To fix spelling in place:

```bash
make spell_fix
```

If codespell is incorrectly flagging a word, you can skip spellcheck for that word by adding it to the codespell config in the `pyproject.toml` file.

```python
[tool.codespell]
...
# Add here:
ignore-words-list = 'momento,collison,ned,foor,reworkd,parth,whats,aapply,mysogyny,unsecure'
```

## Working with Optional Dependencies​

`langchain`, `langchain-community`, and `langchain-experimental` rely on optional dependencies to keep these packages lightweight.

`langchain-core` and partner packages **do not use** optional dependencies in this way.

You'll notice that `pyproject.toml` and `poetry.lock` are **not** touched when you add optional dependencies below.

If you're adding a new dependency to Langchain, assume that it will be an optional dependency, and
that most users won't have it installed.

Users who do not have the dependency installed should be able to **import** your code without
any side effects (no warnings, no errors, no exceptions).

To introduce the dependency to a library, please do the following:

1. Open extended_testing_deps.txt and add the dependency

2. Add a unit test that the very least attempts to import the new code. Ideally, the unit
test makes use of lightweight fixtures to test the logic of the code.

3. Please use the `@pytest.mark.requires(package_name)` decorator for any unit tests that require the dependency.

## Adding a Jupyter Notebook​

If you are adding a Jupyter Notebook example, you'll want to install the optional `dev` dependencies.

To install dev dependencies:

```bash
poetry install --with dev
```

Launch a notebook:

```bash
poetry run jupyter notebook
```

When you run `poetry install`, the `langchain` package is installed as editable in the virtualenv, so your new logic can be imported into the notebook.

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Dependency Management: Poetry and other env/dependency managers](#REDACTED_TOKEN)

metadata.title: How to Set Up a LangGraph Application for Deployment | 🦜🕸️LangGraph

documents.page_content:
How to Set Up a LangGraph Application for Deployment

How to Set Up a LangGraph Application for Deployment¶
A LangGraph application must be configured with a LangGraph API configuration file in order to be deployed to LangGraph Cloud (or to be self-hosted). This how-to guide discusses the basic steps to setup a LangGraph application for deployment using requirements.txt to specify project dependencies.
This walkthrough is based on this repository, which you can play around with to learn more about how to setup your LangGraph application for deployment.

Setup with pyproject.toml
If you prefer using poetry for dependency management, check out this how-to guide on using pyproject.toml for LangGraph Cloud.

Setup with a Monorepo
If you are interested in deploying a graph located inside a monorepo, take a look at this repository for an example of how to do so.

The final repo structure will look something like this:
my-app/
├── my_agent # all project code lies within here
│   ├── utils # utilities for your graph
│   │   ├── __init__.py
│   │   ├── tools.py # tools for your graph
│   │   ├── nodes.py # node functions for you graph
│   │   └── state.py # state definition of your graph
│   ├── requirements.txt # package dependencies
│   ├── __init__.py
│   └── agent.py # code for constructing your graph
├── .env # environment variables
└── langgraph.json # configuration file for LangGraph

After each step, an example file directory is provided to demonstrate how code can be organized.
Specify Dependencies¶
Dependencies can optionally be specified in one of the following files: pyproject.toml, setup.py, or requirements.txt. If none of these files is created, then dependencies can be specified later in the LangGraph API configuration file.
The dependencies below will be included in the image, you can also use them in your code, as long as with a compatible version range:
langgraph>=0.2.56,<0.3.0
langgraph-checkpoint>=2.0.5,<3.0
langchain-core>=0.2.38,<0.4.0
langsmith>=0.1.63
orjson>=3.9.7
httpx>=0.25.0
tenacity>=8.0.0
uvicorn>=0.26.0
sse-starlette>=2.1.0
uvloop>=0.18.0
httptools>=0.5.0
jsonschema-rs>=0.16.3
croniter>=1.0.1
structlog>=23.1.0
redis>=5.0.0,<6.0.0

Example requirements.txt file:
langgraph
langchain_anthropic
tavily-python
langchain_community
langchain_openai

Example file directory:
my-app/
├── my_agent # all project code lies within here
│   └── requirements.txt # package dependencies

Specify Environment Variables¶
Environment variables can optionally be specified in a file (e.g. .env). See the Environment Variables reference to configure additional variables for a deployment.
Example .env file:
MY_ENV_VAR_1=foo
MY_ENV_VAR_2=bar
OPENAI_API_KEY=key

Example file directory:
my-app/
├── my_agent # all project code lies within here
│   └── requirements.txt # package dependencies
└── .env # environment variables

Define Graphs¶
Implement your graphs! Graphs can be defined in a single file or multiple files. Make note of the variable names of each CompiledGraph to be included in the LangGraph application. The variable names will be used later when creating the LangGraph API configuration file.
Example agent.py file, which shows how to import from other modules you define (code for the modules is not shown here, please see this repo to see their implementation):
# my_agent/agent.py
from typing import Literal
from typing_extensions import TypedDict

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END, START
from my_agent.utils.nodes import call_model, should_continue, tool_node # import nodes
from my_agent.utils.state import AgentState # import state

# Define the config
class GraphConfig(TypedDict):
    model_name: Literal["anthropic", "openai"]

documents.page_content:
Setup | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/contributing/how_to/code/setup.mdx)# Setup

This guide walks through how to run the repository locally and check in your first code.
For a [development container](https://containers.dev/), see the [.devcontainer folder](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/tree/master/.devcontainer).

## Dependency Management: Poetry and other env/dependency managers​

This project utilizes [Poetry](https://python-poetry.org/) v1.7.1+ as a dependency manager.

❗Note: _Before installing Poetry_, if you use `Conda`, create and activate a new Conda env (e.g. `conda create -n langchain python=3.9`)

Install Poetry: **documentation on how to install it**.

❗Note: If you use `Conda` or `Pyenv` as your environment/package manager, after installing Poetry,
tell Poetry to use the virtualenv python environment (`poetry config virtualenvs.prefer-active-python true`)

## Different packages​

This repository contains multiple packages:

- `langchain-core`: Base interfaces for key abstractions as well as logic for combining them in chains (LangChain Expression Language).

- `langchain-community`: Third-party integrations of various components.

- `langchain`: Chains, agents, and retrieval logic that makes up the cognitive architecture of your applications.

- `langchain-experimental`: Components and chains that are experimental, either in the sense that the techniques are novel and still being tested, or they require giving the LLM more access than would be possible in most production systems.

- Partner integrations: Partner packages in `libs/partners` that are independently version controlled.

Each of these has its own development environment. Docs are run from the top-level makefile, but development
is split across separate test & release flows.

For this quickstart, start with langchain-community:

```bash
cd libs/community
```

## Local Development Dependencies​

Install langchain-community development requirements (for running langchain, running examples, linting, formatting, tests, and coverage):

```bash
poetry install --with lint,typing,test,test_integration
```

Then verify dependency installation:

```bash
make test
```

If during installation you receive a `WheelFileValidationError` for `debugpy`, please make sure you are running
Poetry v1.6.1+. This bug was present in older versions of Poetry (e.g. 1.4.1) and has been resolved in newer releases.
If you are still seeing this bug on v1.6.1+, you may also try disabling "modern installation"
(`poetry config installer.modern-installation false`) and re-installing requirements.
See [this debugpy issue](https://github.com/microsoft/debugpy/issues/1246) for more details.

## Testing​

**Note:** In `langchain`, `langchain-community`, and `langchain-experimental`, some test dependencies are optional. See the following section about optional dependencies.

Unit tests cover modular logic that does not require calls to outside APIs.
If you add new logic, please add a unit test.

To run unit tests:

```bash
make test
```

To run unit tests in Docker:

```bash
make docker_tests
```

There are also [integration tests and code-coverage](/docs/contributing/how_to/testing/) available.

### Only develop langchain_core or langchain_community​

If you are only developing `langchain_core` or `langchain_community`, you can simply install the dependencies for the respective projects and run tests:

```bash
cd libs/core
poetry install --with test
make test
```

Or:

```bash
cd libs/community
poetry install --with test
make test
```

## Formatting and Linting​

Run these locally before submitting a PR; the CI system will check also.

### Code Formatting​

Formatting for this project is done via [ruff](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/).

To run formatting for docs, cookbook and templates:

```bash
make format
```

documents.page_content:
How to install LangChain packages | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

[Skip to main content](#__docusaurus_skipToContent_fallback)[](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/master/docs/docs/how_to/installation.mdx)# How to install LangChain packages

The LangChain ecosystem is split into different packages, which allow you to choose exactly which pieces of
functionality to install.

## Official release​

To install the main `langchain` package, run:

 
Pip
```bash
pip install langchain
```

Conda
```bash
conda install langchain -c conda-forge
```

While this package acts as a sane starting point to using LangChain,
much of the value of LangChain comes when integrating it with various model providers, datastores, etc.
By default, the dependencies needed to do that are NOT installed. You will need to install the dependencies for specific integrations separately, which we show below.

## Ecosystem packages​

With the exception of the `langsmith` SDK, all packages in the LangChain ecosystem depend on `langchain-core`, which contains base
classes and abstractions that other packages use. The dependency graph below shows how the different packages are related.
A directed arrow indicates that the source package depends on the target package:

![](/assets/images/ecosystem_packages-REDACTED_HASH.png)

When installing a package, you do not need to explicitly install that package's explicit dependencies (such as `langchain-core`).
However, you may choose to if you are using a feature only available in a certain version of that dependency.
If you do, you should make sure that the installed or pinned version is compatible with any other integration packages you use.

### LangChain core​

The `langchain-core` package contains base abstractions that the rest of the LangChain ecosystem uses, along with the LangChain Expression Language. It is automatically installed by `langchain`, but can also be used separately. Install with:

```bash
pip install langchain-core
```

### Integration packages​

Certain integrations like OpenAI and Anthropic have their own packages.
Any integrations that require their own package will be documented as such in the [Integration docs](/docs/integrations/providers/).
You can see a list of all integration packages in the [API reference](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/) under the "Partner libs" dropdown.
To install one of these run:

```bash
pip install langchain-openai
```

Any integrations that haven't been split out into their own packages will live in the `langchain-community` package. Install with:

```bash
pip install langchain-community
```

### LangChain experimental​

The `langchain-experimental` package holds experimental LangChain code, intended for research and experimental uses.
Install with:

```bash
pip install langchain-experimental
```

### LangGraph​

`langgraph` is a library for building stateful, multi-actor applications with LLMs. It integrates smoothly with LangChain, but can be used without it.
Install with:

```bash
pip install langgraph
```

### LangServe​

LangServe helps developers deploy LangChain runnables and chains as a REST API.
LangServe is automatically installed by LangChain CLI.
If not using LangChain CLI, install with:

```bash
pip install "langserve[all]"
```

for both client and server dependencies. Or `pip install "langserve[client]"` for client code, and `pip install "langserve[server]"` for server code.

### LangChain CLI​

The LangChain CLI is useful for working with LangChain templates and other LangServe projects.
Install with:

```bash
pip install langchain-cli
```

### LangSmith SDK​

The LangSmith SDK is automatically installed by LangChain. However, it does not depend on
`langchain-core`, and can be installed and used independently if desired.
If you are not using LangChain, you can install it with:

```bash
pip install langsmith
```

### From source​

## Troubleshooting​

The following may help resolve this error:

- Ensure that all inputs to chat models are an array of LangChain message classes or a supported message-like. 
- Check that there is no stringification or other unexpected transformation occuring.

- Check the error's stack trace and add log or debugger statements.

How to print detailed logs (Python SDK) | 🦜️🛠️ LangSmith

Skip to main contentLearn the essentials of LangSmith in the new Introduction to LangSmith course!  Enroll for free. API ReferenceRESTPythonSearchRegionUSEUGo to AppQuick StartObservabilityTutorialsAdd observability to your LLM applicationHow-to GuidesTracingAnnotate code for tracingToggle tracing on and offUpload files with tracesLog traces to specific projectSet a sampling rate for tracesAdd metadata and tags to tracesImplement distributed tracingAccess the current run (span) within a traced functionLog multimodal tracesLog retriever tracesLog custom LLM tracesPrevent logging of sensitive data in tracesQuery tracesShare or unshare a trace publiclyCompare tracesTrace generator functionsTrace with LangChain (Python and JS/TS)Trace with LangGraph (Python and JS/TS)Trace with Instructor (Python only)Trace with OpenTelemetryTrace with the Vercel AI SDK (JS/TS only)Trace without setting environment variablesTrace using the LangSmith REST APICalculate token-based costs for tracesTroubleshoot trace nesting[Beta] Bulk Exporting Trace DataHow to print detailed logs (Python SDK)Trace JS functions in serverless environmentsMonitoring and automationsConceptual GuideEvaluationPrompt EngineeringDeployment (LangGraph Platform)REDACTED_TOKEN architecture and scalabilityAuthz and AuthnAuthentication methodsdata_formatsEvaluationDataset transformationsRegions FAQsdk_referenceObservabilityHow-to GuidesTracingHow to print detailed logs (Python SDK)On this pageHow to print detailed logs (Python SDK)
The LangSmith package uses Python's built in logging mechanism to output logs
about its behavior to standard output.
Ensure logging is configured​
noteBy default, Jupyter notebooks send logs to standard error instead of standard output, which means your
logs will not show up in your notebook cell output unless you configure logging as we do below.
If logging is not currently configured to send logs to standard output for your Python environment, you'll need to explicitly turn it on as follows:
Pythonimport logging# Note: this will affect _all_ packages that use python's built-in logging mechanism, #       so may increase your log volume. Pick the right log level for your use case.logging.basicConfig(level=logging.WARNING)
Increase the logger's verbosity​
When debugging an issue, it's helpful to increase logs to a higher level verbosity so more info is
outputted to standard output. Python loggers default to using WARNING log level, but you can choose
different values to get different levels of verbosity. The values, from least verbose to most, are ERROR,
WARNING, INFO, and DEBUG. You can set this as follows:
Pythonimport langsmithimport logging# Loggers are hierarchical, so setting the log level on "langsmith" will# set it on all modules inside the "langsmith" packagelangsmith_logger = logging.getLogger("langsmith")langsmith_logger.setLevel(level=logging.DEBUG)Was this page helpful?You can leave detailed feedback on GitHub.Previous[Beta] Bulk Exporting Trace DataNextTrace JS functions in serverless environmentsEnsure logging is configuredIncrease the logger's REDACTED_TOKEN CodeLangSmith SDKPythonJS/TSMoreHomepageBlogLangChain Python DocsLangChain JS/TS DocsCopyright © 2025 LangChain, Inc.

[[GenerationChunk(text='## Python: Pros and Cons

### Pros:

* **Easy to learn:** Python is often cited as one of the easiest programming languages to learn, making it a popular choice for beginners. Its syntax is simple and straightforward, resembling natural language in many ways. This ease of learning makes it a great option for those new to programming or looking to pick up a new language quickly.
* **Versatile:**  Python is a versatile language, suitable for a wide range of applications. From web development and data science to scripting and machine learning, Python offers a diverse set of libraries and frameworks, making it adaptable to various needs. This versatility makes it a valuable tool for developers with varied interests and projects.
* **Cross-platform:** Python can be used on various operating systems, including Windows, macOS, Linux, and Unix. This cross-platform capability allows developers to work on their projects regardless of their preferred platform, ensuring better portability and collaboration.
* **Large community:** Python boasts a vast and active community, providing ample resources for support, learning, and collaboration. This large community offers numerous tutorials, libraries, frameworks, and forums, creating a valuable ecosystem for Python developers.
* **Open-source:** Python is an open-source language, meaning its source code is freely available for anyone to use, modify, and distribute. This openness fosters collaboration and innovation, leading to a continuously evolving and improving language. 
* **Extensive libraries:** Python offers a vast collection of libraries and frameworks, covering diverse areas like data science (NumPy, Pandas, Scikit-learn), web development (Django, Flask), machine learning (TensorFlow, PyTorch), and more. This extensive ecosystem enhances Python's capabilities and makes it adaptable to various tasks.

### Cons:

* **Dynamically typed:** Python uses dynamic typing, where variable types are determined at runtime. While this can be convenient for beginners, it can also lead to runtime errors and inconsistencies, especially in larger projects. Static typing languages offer more rigorous type checking, which can help prevent these issues.
* **Slow execution speed:** Compared to compiled languages like C++ or Java, Python is generally slower due to its interpreted nature. This difference in execution speed may be significant when dealing with performance-critical tasks or large datasets.
* **"Not invented here" syndrome:** Python's popularity has sometimes led to the "not invented here" syndrome, where developers might reject external libraries or frameworks in favor of creating their own solutions. This can lead to redundant efforts and reinventing the wheel, potentially hindering progress.
* **Global Interpreter Lock (GIL):** Python's GIL limits the use of multiple CPU cores effectively, as only one thread can execute Python bytecode at a time. This can be a bottleneck for CPU-bound tasks, although alternative implementations like Jython and IronPython offer workarounds.

Overall, Python's strengths lie in its ease of learning, versatility, and large community, making it a popular choice for various applications. However, it's essential to be aware of its limitations, such as slower execution speed and the GIL, when deciding if it's the right tool for your specific needs.', generation_info={'is_blocked': False, 'safety_ratings': [{'category': 'HARM_CATEGORY_HATE_SPEECH', 'probability_label': 'NEGLIGIBLE', 'blocked': False}, {'category': 'HARM_CATEGORY_DANGEROUS_CONTENT', 'probability_label': 'NEGLIGIBLE', 'blocked': False}, {'category': 'HARM_CATEGORY_HARASSMENT', 'probability_label': 'NEGLIGIBLE', 'blocked': False}, {'category': 'HARM_CATEGORY_SEXUALLY_EXPLICIT', 'probability_label': 'NEGLIGIBLE', 'blocked': False}], 'citation_metadata': None, 'usage_metadata': {'prompt_token_count': 15, 'candidates_token_count': 647, 'total_token_count':

documents.page_content:
```python
message = "What are some of the pros and cons of Python as a programming language?"
model.invoke(message)
```

```output
"## Pros of Python:

* **Easy to learn and use:** Python's syntax is simple and straightforward, making it a great choice for beginners. 
* **Extensive library support:** Python has a massive collection of libraries and frameworks for a variety of tasks, from web development to data science. 
* **Open source and free:** Anyone can use and contribute to Python without paying licensing fees.
* **Large and active community:** There's a vast community of Python users offering help and support.
* **Versatility:** Python is a general-purpose language, meaning it can be used for a wide variety of tasks.
* **Portable and cross-platform:** Python code works seamlessly across various operating systems.
* **High-level language:** Python hides many of the complexities of lower-level languages, allowing developers to focus on problem solving.
* **Readability:** The clear syntax makes Python programs easier to understand and maintain, especially for collaborative projects.

## Cons of Python:

* **Slower execution:** Compared to compiled languages like C++, Python is generally slower due to its interpreted nature.
* **Dynamically typed:** Python doesn’t enforce strict data types, which can sometimes lead to errors.
* **Global Interpreter Lock (GIL):** The GIL limits Python to using a single CPU core at a time, impacting its performance in multi-core environments.
* **Large memory footprint**: Python programs require more memory than some other languages.
* **Not ideal for low-level programming:** Python is not suitable for tasks requiring direct hardware interaction.



## Conclusion:

While it has some drawbacks, Python's strengths outweigh them, making it a very versatile and approachable programming language for beginners. Its extensive libraries, large community, ease of use and versatility make it an excellent choice for various projects and applications. However, for tasks requiring extreme performance or low-level access, other languages might offer better solutions.
"
```

await model.ainvoke(message)
```

documents.page_content:
```python
await model.ainvoke(message)
```

```output
"## Pros of Python:

* **Easy to learn and read:** Python's syntax is known for its simplicity and readability. Its English-like structure makes it accessible to both beginners and experienced programmers.
* **Versatile:** Python can be used for a wide range of applications, from web development and data science to machine learning and automation. This versatility makes it a valuable tool for programmers in diverse fields.
* **Large and active community:** Python has a massive and passionate community of users, developers, and contributors. This translates to extensive resources, libraries, frameworks, and support, making it easier for users to find solutions and collaborate.
* **Rich libraries and frameworks:** Python boasts an extensive ecosystem of open-source libraries and frameworks for various tasks, including data analysis, web development, machine learning, and scientific computing. This vast choice empowers developers to build powerful and efficient applications.
* **Cross-platform compatibility:** Python runs on various operating systems like Windows, macOS, Linux, and Unix, making it a portable and adaptable language for development. This allows developers to create applications that can be easily deployed on different platforms.
* **High-level abstraction:** Python's high-level nature allows developers to focus on the logic of their programs rather than low-level details like memory management. This abstraction contributes to faster development and cleaner code.

## Cons of Python:

* **Slow execution speed:** Compared to languages like C or C++, Python is generally slower due to its interpreted nature. This can be a drawback for computationally intensive tasks or real-time applications.
* **Dynamic typing:** While dynamic typing offers flexibility, it can lead to runtime errors that might go unnoticed during development. This can be particularly challenging for large and complex projects.
* **Global interpreter lock (GIL):** Python's GIL limits the performance of multi-threaded applications. It only allows one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time, which can hamper parallel processing capabilities.
* **Memory management:** Python handles memory management automatically, which can lead to memory leaks in certain cases. Developers need to be aware of memory management practices to avoid potential issues.
* **Limited hardware control:** Python's design prioritizes ease of use and portability over low-level hardware control. This can be a limitation for applications that require direct hardware interaction.

Overall, Python offers a strong balance between ease of use, versatility, and a rich ecosystem. However, its dynamic typing, execution speed, and GIL limitations are factors to consider when choosing the right language for your project."
```

metadata.description: The FileReader class provides methods for loading files from storage into generic Document objects. Here we demonstrate:

documents.page_content:
If you need to load Python source code files, use the `PythonLoader`:

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PythonLoader

loader = DirectoryLoader("../../../../../", glob="**/*.py", loader_cls=PythonLoader)
```

**API Reference:**[PythonLoader](https://python.langchain.com/api_reference/community/document_loaders/langchain_community.document_loaders.python.PythonLoader.html)
## Auto-detect file encodings with TextLoader​

`DirectoryLoader` can help manage errors due to variations in file encodings. Below we will attempt to load in a collection of files, one of which includes non-UTF8 encodings.

```python
path = "../../../libs/langchain/tests/unit_tests/examples/"

loader = DirectoryLoader(path, glob="**/*.txt", loader_cls=TextLoader)
```

### A. Default Behavior​

By default we raise an error:

```python
loader.load()
```

```output

RuntimeError: Error loading ../../../../libs/langchain/tests/unit_tests/examples/example-non-utf8.txt
```

The file `example-non-utf8.txt` uses a different encoding, so the `load()` function fails with a helpful message indicating which file failed decoding.

metadata.description: The latest version of the software was released in the spring of 2024. This update introduces several significant changes and deprecated features. This document provides a guide for upgrading to the latest version.
metadata.title: Migration | 🦜️🔗 LangChain

documents.page_content:
```bash
# Run a first time
# Will replace from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI
langchain-cli migrate --diff [path to code] # Preview
langchain-cli migrate [path to code] # Apply

# Run a second time to apply more import replacements
langchain-cli migrate --diff [path to code] # Preview
langchain-cli migrate [path to code] # Apply
```

### Other options​

```bash
# See help menu
langchain-cli migrate --help
# Preview Changes without applying
langchain-cli migrate --diff [path to code]
# Run on code including ipython notebooks
# Apply all import updates except for updates from langchain to langchain-core
langchain-cli migrate --disable langchain_to_core --include-ipynb [path to code]
```

#### Was this page helpful?

- [Upgrade to new imports](#upgrade-to-new-imports)

- [Installation](#installation)

- [Usage](#usage)- [Other options](#other-options)

This error occurs because the `langchain-chroma` package is not installed. Here's how to resolve it:

1. Install the Package:
```bash
pip install langchain-chroma
```

2. Update your requirements.txt:
Add this line to your requirements.txt:
```
langchain-chroma>=0.1.0
```

3. Full Installation Command:
```bash
pip install langchain-chroma
```

4. Complete Import Example:
```python
# Correct import
from langchain_chroma import Chroma

# If using with embeddings
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

# Example usage
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()
vectorstore = Chroma.from_texts(
    texts=["hello", "world"],
    embedding=embeddings
)
```

5. Troubleshooting Steps:
```bash
# Upgrade pip
python -m pip install --upgrade pip

# Clean install
pip install --no-cache-dir langchain-chroma

# Verify installation
pip show langchain-chroma
```

6. Updated Requirements Snippet:
```
langchain==0.3.13
langchain-core==0.3.28
langchain-community==0.3.13
langchain-openai==0.2.14
langchain-chroma>=0.1.0
python-dotenv==1.0.1
```

7. Comprehensive Installation:
```bash
pip install \
    langchain==0.3.13 \
    langchain-core==0.3.28 \
    langchain-community==0.3.13 \
    langchain-openai==0.2.14 \
    langchain-chroma \
    python-dotenv
```

Common Alternatives if Still Facing Issues:
```python
# Alternative 1: Use Chroma from community
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

# Alternative 2: Direct Chroma import
import chromadb
from chromadb.config import Settings

# Create Chroma client
chroma_client = chromadb.Client(Settings(
    chroma_db_impl="duckdb+parquet",
    persist_directory="./chroma_db"
))
```

Verification Script:
```python
# Verify Chroma Installation
try:
    from langchain_chroma import Chroma
    print("Chroma installed successfully!")
except ImportError as e:
    print(f"Installation failed: {e}")
    print("Please run: pip install langchain-chroma")
```

Potential Additional Dependencies:
```bash
pip install \
    chromadb \
    numpy \
    openai
```

If Persistent Issues:
1. Check Python version compatibility
2. Ensure all dependencies are up-to-date
3. Create a fresh virtual environment
4. Verify network connection

Recommended Environment Setup:
```bash
# Create virtual environment
python -m venv langchain_env
source langchain_env/bin/activate  # On Windows: langchain_env\Scripts\activate

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Verify installations
pip list | grep langchain
```

Debugging Checklist:

- ✅ Update requirements.txt
- ✅ Check Python version
- ✅ Verify dependency versions
- ✅ Use virtual environment

You are an expert in a specialized software framework and a top-tier researcher, here to assist with any questions or issues related to the framework, its associated tools, or related functionalities. Users may approach you with inquiries or problems.

Based on the conversation below, develop a plan for how you will investigate the answer to their question.

The plan should generally be no longer than three steps; it can be as brief as one. The length of the plan depends on the complexity of the question.

You have access to the following documentation sources:
- Conceptual reference materials
- Integration guides
- Practical how-to tutorials

You do not need to specify the exact sources for each step, but providing some guidance can be helpful.

I have this code - prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Please provide a humorous remark about {subject}")

message  = prompt_template.invoke({"subject": "animals"})

chatModel.invoke(message) --- and I have this code ---> prompt_template = "Convert the following English sentence to another language: {sentence}"

prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)

formatted_prompt = prompt.format(sentence="Good morning, I am a visitor.")
formatted_prompt --- Why do we use invoke twice in the above example, and how is it different from the following? Which method is generally preferred?

Certainly! Here's a fully anonymized version of your text, maintaining the original structure, length, and intent, while replacing all personal, software-specific, and sensitive details with generic placeholders:

---

Let me clarify the differences between these two methods and explain the purpose of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a humorous remark about {subject}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"subject": "animals"})
model.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following text from Language A to Language B: {content}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(content="Greetings, I am a user.")
formatted_prompt
```

Main Differences:

1. Formatting Approach:
- First example uses `invoke()`: This method formats the template and produces a complete message object.
- Second example uses `format()`: This method replaces the placeholder with the provided value directly.

2. Return Type:
- `invoke()` yields a `PromptObject`
- `format()` results in a plain string

3. Recommended Method:
The first method (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in the context of this framework because:
- It aligns with the chain-based processing workflow
- It produces a standardized `PromptObject` suitable for direct input to models
- It offers greater flexibility and integrates smoothly into the overall processing pipeline

Suggested Modern Practice:
```python
# Recommended approach
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a humorous remark about {subject}")
chain = prompt | model  # Using the framework's expression syntax
result = chain.invoke({"subject": "animals"})
```

This method:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Constructs a complete processing chain
- Facilitates modular component composition
- Is the idiomatic approach in recent versions of the framework

Detailed Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than simple string substitution
- It creates a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata related to the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Example to highlight the difference:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(content="Hello")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_obj = prompt.invoke({"content": "Hello"})
print(type(prompt_obj))  # <class 'PromptObject'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working within the framework's chain architecture
- Use `format()` for straightforward string templating without model interaction
- Adopt the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for most modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` when integrating with models and chain construction
2. Use `format()` for simple string formatting tasks
3. Prefer the pipe (`|`) operator for building processing chains

---

Let me know if you'd like any further adjustments!

My initial code snippet is as follows:
```python
messages = chat_template.format_messages(
    profession="film director",
    topic="comedy film",
    user_input="suggest some popular comedy movies"
)
```
The second snippet is:
```python
response = chatModel.invoke(messages)
print(response.content)
```
It provides the correct answer. However, when I try to replicate it using a different framework, I use:
```python
chain = chat_template | llmModel
response = chain.invoke({"profession":"film director", "topic":"comedy film", "user_input": "suggest popular comedy movies"})
print(response.content)
```
The response is the same as before. Why is this happening, and how can I resolve it?

Certainly! Here's a fully anonymized and generalized version of your text, maintaining the original structure, length, and intent:

---

Let me clarify the distinctions and suggest a solution:

The primary difference lies in how the prompts are structured and supplied to the language model:

1. First Method (Works as Intended):
```python
messages = prompt_template.format_messages(
    role="content creator",
    subject="humorous film",
    user_input="suggest some names of popular comedy movies"
)
response = language_model.invoke(messages)
```

2. Alternative Approach (Potential Issue):
```python
pipeline = prompt_template | language_model
response = pipeline.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous film",
    "user_input": "recommend top-grossing comedy movies in a specific language"
})
```

The challenge is that the second approach may not automatically convert the input into the required message format for the chat model. Here are some possible solutions:

**Solution 1: Use a ChatPromptTemplate**
```python
from some_prompt_library import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define the chat prompt template
prompt_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in {role}."),
    ("user", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("user", "{user_input}")
])

# Build the chain with the prompt template
pipeline = prompt_template | language_model

# Invoke the chain
response = pipeline.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous film",
    "user_input": "recommend top-grossing comedy movies in a specific language"
})
print(response.content)
```

**Solution 2: Explicitly Convert Inputs to Messages**
```python
from some_library import RunnablePassThrough
from output_parsers import StringParser

# Create a chain that formats messages before passing to the model
chain = (
    RunnablePassThrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: prompt_template.format_messages(**x)
    )
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract the message list
    | language_model
    | StringParser()
)

response = chain.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous film",
    "user_input": "recommend top-grossing comedy movies in a specific language"
})
print(response)
```

**Solution 3: For Chat Models (e.g., OpenAI's chat APIs)**
```python
from some_chat_library import ChatModel

# Ensure the model is a chat-compatible one
chat_model = ChatModel(model="model-name")

# Build the chain
pipeline = prompt_template | chat_model

response = pipeline.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous film",
    "user_input": "recommend top-grossing comedy movies in a specific language"
})
print(response.content)
```

**Key Points to Remember:**
1. The initial method explicitly creates message objects.
2. The alternative approach (LCEL) requires correct message formatting.
3. Different model types (chat vs. text completion) process inputs differently.

**Debugging Tips:**
- Confirm that your prompt template is a `ChatPromptTemplate` or equivalent.
- Verify that your model supports chat input.
- Print the generated messages to check their structure.

**Recommended Best Practice:**
```python
# Most reliable method
from some_prompt_library import ChatPromptTemplate
from some_chat_library import ChatModel

# Define your message template
prompt_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in {role}."),
    ("user", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("user", "{user_input}")
])

# Instantiate the chat model
chat_model = ChatModel(model="model-name")

# Create the chain
pipeline = prompt_template | chat_model

# Invoke with consistent parameters
response = pipeline.invoke({
    "role": "film director",
    "subject": "humorous film",
    "user_input": "recommend top-grossing comedy movies in a specific language"
})
print(response.content)
```

This method guarantees:
- Proper message formatting
- Uniform input handling
- Compatibility with chat-based models
- Flexible chain composition

---

Let me know if you'd like further adjustments!

from generic_module.runnables import RunnablePassthrough
from generic_module.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

# Create a processing chain that first formats messages
chain = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: generic_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract just the messages
    | language_model
    | StrOutputParser()
)

response = chain.invoke({
    "profession": "film Director", 
    "topic": "comedy movie", 
    "user_input": "please provide a list of popular comedy movies"
})
print(response)
 -- this worked, please tell me what was the issue with my code so that this won't be repeated

Let's analyze the issues with the original code and clarify why the revised approach is effective:

Potential Issues in the Original Code:
1. Direct Conversion of Chat Templates
```python
workflow = chat_template | language_model
response = workflow.invoke({...})
```
The main concern here is that the system doesn't automatically translate your template parameters into a valid chat message sequence.

Detailed Explanation of the Corrected Approach:
```python
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract the formatted messages
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Step-by-step Breakdown:

1. `RunnablePassthrough.assign()`:
   - Creates a new dictionary that retains all original inputs
   - Adds a new key `messages`
   - Uses `format_messages()` to convert input data into properly formatted chat messages
   - Ensures the message template is correctly prepared before passing to the model

2. `lambda x: x['messages']`:
   - Retrieves only the list of formatted chat messages
   - Guarantees that only the chat content is sent to the language model

3. `| language_model`:
   - Feeds the prepared messages into the language processing model

4. `| StringOutputParser()`:
   - Converts the model's output into a simple string format

Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
1. Assuming direct template conversion works universally
2. Confusing prompt templates with chat message formatting
3. Mixing different types of models (e.g., text completion vs. chat models)

Recommended Best Practices:
```python
# Always utilize ChatPromptTemplate for chat-based models
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define a chat-specific prompt template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an {occupation} specialist."),
    ("human", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("human", "{user_input}")
])

# Build a resilient processing chain
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Alternative Robust Methods:
1. Using `RunnableLambda`:
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def format_messages(input_dict):
    return chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(format_messages)
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

2. Explicit Message Preparation:
```python
def prepare_messages(input_dict):
    return {
        "messages": chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)
    }

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(prepare_messages)
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Debugging Strategies:
1. Print intermediate outputs
2. Confirm message formatting correctness
3. Ensure model compatibility with message format

Example Debugging Snippet:
```python
# Sample input for debugging
test_input = {
    "occupation": "software engineer", 
    "subject": "project management", 
    "user_input": "Can you suggest best practices for team collaboration?"
}

# Check formatted messages
formatted_msgs = chat_template.format_messages(**test_input)
print("Formatted Messages:")
for msg in formatted_msgs:
    print(f"Type: {type(msg)}, Content: {msg}")
```

Key Takeaways:
- Chat models need specifically formatted message sequences
- Flexible tools exist to prepare these messages effectively
- Always verify message conversion steps
- Use debugging to troubleshoot message formatting issues

The approach you shared is effective because it:
1. Converts input data into chat-compatible messages
2. Extracts those messages
3. Sends them to the language model
4. Parses the response into a simple string

Understanding these steps helps in designing more adaptable and reliable workflows within the framework.

Certainly! Here's an anonymized version of the provided text, maintaining structure, length, and format, while replacing all personal, specific, and sensitive details with generic placeholders:

---

I'll provide you with the latest few-shot prompting templates for both conversational models and text completion models, based on the most recent documentation:

1. Few-Shot Prompting for Chat-Based Models:
```python
from core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate
from provider_module import ChatModel

# Define your example cases
examples = [
    {"input": "A 🦜 B", "output": "C"},
    {"input": "A 🦜 D", "output": "E"},
    {"input": "F 🦜 F", "output": "G"}
]

# Create an example selector (optional, but recommended)
from core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from storage_module import StorageBackend
from provider_module import EmbeddingModel

example_selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    EmbeddingModel(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Number of examples to select
)

# Define the few-shot prompt template
few_shot_prompt = FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate(
    # The input variables specify which values to pass to the selector
    input_variables=["input"],
    example_selector=example_selector,
    # Define how each example will be formatted
    example_prompt=ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages(
        [("user", "{input}"), ("assistant", "{output}")]
    ),
)

# Create the final prompt template
final_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in specialized operations."),
    few_shot_prompt,
    ("user", "{input}")
])

# Create the chain
model = ChatModel(model="model-variant")
chain = final_prompt | model

# Run the chain
result = chain.invoke({"input": "What is A 🦜 B?"})
print(result.content)
```

2. Few-Shot Prompting for Text Completion Models:
```python
from core.prompts import PromptTemplate, FewShotPromptTemplate
from provider_module import TextModel

# Define your example questions and answers
examples = [
    {"question": "What is the capital of CountryX?", "answer": "CityY"},
    {"question": "Who created the famous artwork?", "answer": "ArtistName"},
    {"question": "What is the largest celestial body?", "answer": "PlanetZ"}
]

# Create an example selector
from core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from storage_module import StorageBackend
from provider_module import EmbeddingModel

example_selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    EmbeddingModel(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Number of examples to select
)

# Define the example prompt
example_prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["question", "answer"],
    template="Question: {question}\nAnswer: {answer}"
)

# Create the few-shot prompt template
few_shot_prompt = FewShotPromptTemplate(
    example_selector=example_selector,
    example_prompt=example_prompt,
    suffix="Question: {input}\nAnswer:",
    input_variables=["input"],
    prefix="Please answer the following questions clearly and accurately:"
)

# Create the chain
model = TextModel(model="model-variant")
chain = few_shot_prompt | model

# Run the chain
result = chain.invoke({"input": "Who discovered gravity?"})
print(result)
```

3. Dynamic Few-Shot Prompting (More Advanced):
```python
from core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from core.example_selectors import MaxMarginalRelevanceExampleSelector
from storage_module import StorageBackend
from provider_module import EmbeddingModel, ChatModel

# Additional setup and implementation details...
```

Great inquiry! Let me explain why employing more sophisticated few-shot prompting strategies is valuable and what additional functionalities they enable:

1. Basic Method (Sample Code):
```python
sample_pairs = [
    {"input": "greeting!", "output": "Hello!"},
    {"input": "farewell!", "output": "Goodbye!"},
]
```
Advantages:
- Straightforward to implement
- Suitable for small, predefined examples
- Easy to comprehend

Limitations:
- Fixed set of samples
- No ability to select dynamically
- Lacks semantic relevance
- Performance may decline with larger sample collections

2. Enhanced Method with Sample Selectors:
```python
sample_selector = SemanticSimilaritySampleSelector.from_samples(
    sample_pairs,
    EmbeddingModel(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Select most relevant samples dynamically
)
```

Main Benefits:

A. Semantic Similarity-Based Selection:

B. Scalability:

C. Diversity and Relevance:

    sample_pairs,

    k=2  # Pick 2 samples that are relevant yet diverse
)
```

D. Performance Optimization:

    "Diversity",            # Varied samples

3. Complete Example:

)

)

    ]
)

    semantic_chain = final_semantic_prompt | model
    semantic_response = semantic_chain.invoke({"input": input_text})

    # Using Maximal Marginal Relevance
    print("Maximal Marginal Relevance Approach:")
    mmr_chain = final_mmr_prompt | model

# Test the selectors

Let me clarify the differences between these two approaches and explain the usage of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Snippet:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {subject}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"subject": "birds"})
languageModel.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Snippet:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following English sentence to Spanish: {sentence}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(sentence="Good morning, I am a traveler.")
formatted_prompt
```

Key Differences:

1. Method of Formatting:
- The first snippet uses `invoke()`: This method both formats the template and constructs a complete message object.
- The second snippet uses `format()`: This method replaces the placeholder with the provided value directly.

2. Return Type:
- `invoke()` returns a `PromptValue` object
- `format()` returns a string

3. Recommended Approach:
The first approach (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in frameworks similar to this because:
- It aligns better with the chain-based processing workflow
- It produces a standardized `PromptValue` that can be directly supplied to language models
- It offers greater flexibility and is integrated into the main processing pipeline

Suggested Modern Method:
```python
# Recommended method
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {subject}")
chain = prompt | languageModel  # Using a chain expression syntax
result = chain.invoke({"subject": "birds"})
```

This method:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Builds a complete processing chain
- Facilitates component composition
- Is the most idiomatic approach in recent versions of the framework

Detailed Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than simple string substitution
- It constructs a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata related to the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Example to illustrate the difference:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(sentence="Good morning")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_value = prompt.invoke({"sentence": "Good morning"})
print(type(prompt_value))  # <class 'PromptValue'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working within a chain or pipeline involving models
- Use `format()` for straightforward string templating without model interaction
- Adopt the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for most modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` when integrating with language models or chains
2. Use `format()` for simple string replacements
3. Prefer the pipe (`|`) operator for constructing processing chains

Let's analyze the issues with the original code and clarify why the revised approach is effective:

Potential Issues in the Original Code:
1. Direct Conversion from a General Chain
```python
workflow = chat_template | language_model
response = workflow.invoke({...})
```
The main concern here is that the conversion framework doesn't automatically translate your template parameters into a correctly formatted chat message sequence.

Detailed Explanation of the Improved Solution:
```python
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Extract only the formatted messages
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Step-by-step Breakdown:

1. `RunnablePassthrough.assign()`:
   - Creates a new dictionary that retains all original inputs
   - Adds a new key `messages`
   - Uses `format_messages()` to transform input data into a sequence of chat messages
   - Ensures the message format aligns with what the model expects

2. `lambda x: x['messages']`:
   - Retrieves only the list of formatted chat messages
   - Ensures that only properly formatted messages are sent to the language model

3. `| language_model`:
   - Sends the prepared messages to the language processing model

4. `| StringOutputParser()`:
   - Converts the model's output into a simple string format

Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
1. Assuming direct conversion works universally for all templates
2. Confusing prompt templates with chat message formats
3. Combining text completion models with chat-specific models without proper formatting

Recommended Best Practices:
```python
# Always utilize ChatPromptTemplate for chat-based models
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define a chat-specific prompt template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an {expertise} specialist."),
    ("human", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("human", "{user_input}")
])

# Build a reliable processing chain
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Alternative Robust Methods:
1. Using `RunnableLambda`:
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def format_messages(input_dict):
    return chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(format_messages)
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

2. Explicit Message Preparation:
```python
def prepare_messages(input_dict):
    return {
        "messages": chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)
    }

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(prepare_messages)
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | language_model
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Debugging Strategies:
1. Print intermediate outputs to verify correctness
2. Confirm message formatting aligns with expectations
3. Ensure the model supports the message structure

Example Debugging Snippet:
```python
# Sample input for debugging
test_input = {
    "expertise": "software engineer", 
    "subject": "project management", 
    "user_input": "Can you suggest best practices for team collaboration?"
}

# Check the formatted messages
formatted = chat_template.format_messages(**test_input)
print("Formatted Messages:")
for msg in formatted:
    print(f"Type: {type(msg)}, Content: {msg}")
```

Key Takeaways:
- Chat models require messages to be formatted in a specific way
- Frameworks like LCEL facilitate flexible message preparation
- Always verify message formatting before passing to the model
- Use debugging tools to troubleshoot message construction

I'll provide you with the latest few-shot prompting templates for both conversational models and text completion models, based on the most recent documentation:

1. Few-Shot Prompting for Conversational Models:
```python
from core_prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate
from model_integration import ChatModel

# Define your example cases
examples = [
    {"input": "Expression 1", "output": "Result 1"},
    {"input": "Expression 2", "output": "Result 2"},
    {"input": "Expression 3", "output": "Result 3"}
]

# Create an example selector (optional, but recommended)
from core_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from data_storage import DataStore
from embedding_service import EmbeddingService

example_selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    EmbeddingService(),
    DataStore,
    k=2  # Number of examples to select
)

# Define the few-shot prompt template
few_shot_prompt = FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate(
    # The input variables specify which values to pass to the selector
    input_variables=["input"],
    example_selector=example_selector,
    # Define how each example will be formatted
    example_prompt=ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages(
        [("user", "{input}"), ("assistant", "{output}")]
    ),
)

# Create the final prompt template
final_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in solving complex problems with specialized operators."),
    few_shot_prompt,
    ("user", "{input}")
])

# Create the processing chain
model = ChatModel(model="advanced-chat-model")

Certainly! Here's a fully anonymized version of your text, maintaining the original structure, length, and intent, while replacing all personal, software-specific, and sensitive details with generic placeholders:

---

Let me clarify the differences between these two methods and explain the purpose of `invoke()`:

1. First Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
message = prompt_template.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
model.invoke(message)
```

2. Second Code Example:
```python
prompt_template = "Convert the following text from language A to language B: {text}"
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template(prompt_template)
formatted_prompt = prompt.format(text="Greetings, I am a user.")
formatted_prompt
```

Main Differences:

1. Formatting Approach:
- First example uses `invoke()`: This method formats the template and produces a complete message object.
- Second example uses `format()`: This method replaces the placeholder with the provided value directly.

2. Return Type:
- `invoke()` yields a `PromptObject`
- `format()` results in a plain string

3. Recommended Method:
The first method (using `invoke()`) is generally favored in the context of the framework because:
- It aligns with the chain-based processing workflow
- It produces a standardized `PromptObject` suitable for input to models
- It offers greater flexibility and integrates smoothly into the overall processing pipeline

Suggested Modern Pattern:
```python
# Recommended approach
prompt = PromptTemplate.from_template("Generate a fun fact about {topic}")
chain = prompt | languageModel  # Using a chain expression syntax
result = chain.invoke({"topic": "animals"})
```

This pattern:
- Utilizes the pipe (`|`) operator
- Constructs a complete processing chain
- Facilitates modular component composition
- Is the idiomatic style in recent versions of the framework

Detailed Explanation of `invoke()`:
- `invoke()` does more than simple string substitution
- It creates a structured message that includes:
  - The formatted prompt
  - Metadata related to the prompt
  - Compatibility with various model interfaces

Example to highlight the difference:
```python
# Using format()
formatted_text = prompt.format(text="Hello")
print(type(formatted_text))  # <class 'str'>

# Using invoke()
prompt_obj = prompt.invoke({"text": "Hello"})
print(type(prompt_obj))  # <class 'PromptObject'>
```

When to Use Each:
- Use `invoke()` when working within the framework's chain and component system
- Use `format()` for straightforward string templating without model interaction
- Adopt the chain syntax (`prompt | model`) for most modern workflows

Best Practices:
1. Use `invoke()` when engaging with models and chain construction
2. Use `format()` for simple string replacements
3. Prefer the pipe (`|`) operator for building processing chains

The challenge is that the second approach may not automatically convert the input into the required message format for chat-based models. Here are some possible solutions:

**Solution 1: Use a ChatPromptTemplate Class**
```python
from framework_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define your chat prompt template
prompt_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are a {role} expert."),
    ("user", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("user", "{user_query}")
])

# Build the chain with the pipeline

```python
from framework_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough

Let's analyze the issues with the original code and clarify why the revised approach is effective:

Potential Issues in the Original Code:
1. Direct Conversion from a General Template
```python
workflow = chat_template | languageModel
response = workflow.invoke({...})
```
The main concern here is that the conversion tool doesn't automatically translate your template parameters into a correctly formatted chat message sequence.

Detailed Explanation of the Improved Solution:
```python
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])  # Select only the formatted messages
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Step-by-step Breakdown:

1. `RunnablePassthrough.assign()`:
   - Creates a new dictionary that retains all original inputs
   - Adds a key `messages`
   - Uses `format_messages()` to transform input data into a sequence of chat messages
   - Ensures the message format aligns with what the model expects

2. `lambda x: x['messages']`:
   - Extracts just the list of formatted chat messages
   - Ensures only the properly formatted messages are sent to the language model

3. `| languageModel`:
   - Sends the chat messages to the language processing model

4. `| StringOutputParser()`:
   - Converts the model's output into a simple string format

Common Pitfalls to Avoid:
1. Assuming direct conversion works universally for all templates
2. Confusing prompt templates with chat message formats
3. Combining text completion models with chat-specific models

Recommended Best Practices:
```python
# Always utilize ChatPromptTemplate for chat-based models
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate

# Define a chat-specific prompt template
chat_template = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert in {field}."),
    ("human", "Discuss a {subject}"),
    ("human", "{user_input}")
])

# Build a resilient processing chain
workflow = (
    RunnablePassthrough.assign(
        messages=lambda x: chat_template.format_messages(**x)
    ) 
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Alternative Robust Methods:
1. Using `RunnableLambda`:
```python
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

def format_messages(input_dict):
    return chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(format_messages)
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

2. Explicit Message Preparation:
```python
def prepare_messages(input_dict):
    return {
        "messages": chat_template.format_messages(**input_dict)
    }

workflow = (
    RunnableLambda(prepare_messages)
    | (lambda x: x['messages'])
    | languageModel
    | StringOutputParser()
)
```

Debugging Recommendations:
1. Print intermediate outputs to verify transformations
2. Confirm message formatting aligns with expectations
3. Ensure model compatibility with message formats

- Chat models require messages to be formatted in a specific way
- Flexible tools exist to prepare these messages effectively
- Always verify message formatting during development
- Debugging helps ensure the process works as intended

1. Few-Shot Prompting for Conversational Models:
```python
from core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate
from provider_module import ChatModel

# Define your examples
examples = [
    {"input": "Example Input 1", "output": "Example Output 1"},
    {"input": "Example Input 2", "output": "Example Output 2"},
    {"input": "Example Input 3", "output": "Example Output 3"}
]

# Create an example selector (optional, but recommended)
from core.example_selectors import SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
from storage_module import StorageBackend
from provider_module import EmbeddingsProvider

example_selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    EmbeddingsProvider(),
    StorageBackend,
    k=2  # Number of examples to select
)

# Define the few-shot prompt template
few_shot_prompt = FewShotChatMessagePromptTemplate(
    # The input variables select the values to pass to the example_selector
    input_variables=["input"],
    example_selector=example_selector,
    # Define how each example will be formatted
    example_prompt=ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages(
        [("user", "{input}"), ("assistant", "{output}")]
    ),
)

# Create the final prompt template
final_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ("system", "You are an expert assistant specialized in solving complex problems."),
    few_shot_prompt,
    ("user", "{input}")
])

# Create the chain
model = ChatModel(model="model-identifier")
chain = final_prompt | model

# Invoke the chain
result = chain.invoke({"input": "Sample question for the model?"})
print(result.content)
```

)

    input_variables=["question", "answer"],
    template="Question: {question}\nAnswer: {answer}"
)

# Create the few-shot prompt template

    example_selector=example_selector,
    example_prompt=example_prompt,
    suffix="Question: {input}\nAnswer:",
    input_variables=["input"],
    prefix="Please answer the following questions clearly and accurately:"
)

chain = few_shot_prompt | model

pip install library-package
```

2. Update your requirements.txt:
Add this line to your requirements.txt:
```
library-package>=0.1.0
```

3. Full Installation Command:
```bash
pip install library-package
```

4. Complete Import Example:
```python
# Correct import
from library_module import MainClass

# If using with specific features
from external_module import FeatureEmbeddings

# Example usage
embeddings = FeatureEmbeddings()
vectorstore = MainClass.from_texts(
    texts=["sample1", "sample2"],
    embedding=embeddings
)
```

5. Troubleshooting Steps:
```bash
# Upgrade pip
python -m pip install --upgrade pip

# Clean install
pip install --no-cache-dir library-package

# Verify installation
pip show library-package
```

6. Updated Requirements Snippet:
```
main-library==0.3.13
library-core==0.3.28
library-community==0.3.13
library-external==0.2.14
library-package>=0.1.0
python-dotenv==1.0.1
```

7. Comprehensive Installation:
```bash
pip install \
    main-library==0.3.13 \
    library-core==0.3.28 \
    library-community==0.3.13 \
    library-external==0.2.14 \
    library-package \
    python-dotenv
```

Common Alternatives if Still Facing Issues:
```python
# Alternative 1: Use package from community repository
from community_module.vectorstores import MainClass

# Alternative 2: Direct package import
import externaldb
from externaldb.config import Settings

# Create client instance
client = externaldb.Client(Settings(
    db_impl="database+format",
    persist_directory="./storage_dir"
))
```

Verification Script:
```python
# Verify package installation
try:
    from library_module import MainClass
    print("Package installed successfully!")
except ImportError as e:
    print(f"Installation failed: {e}")
    print("Please run: pip install library-package")
```

Potential Additional Dependencies:
```bash
pip install \
    externaldb \
    numpy \
    someapi
```

If Persistent Issues:
1. Check Python version compatibility
2. Ensure all dependencies are up-to-date
3. Create a fresh virtual environment
4. Verify network connection

Recommended Environment Setup:
```bash
# Create virtual environment
python -m venv env_name
source env_name/bin/activate  # On Windows: env_name\Scripts\activate

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Verify installations
pip list | grep library
```

Debugging Checklist:
- ✅ Install `library-package`
- ✅ Update requirements.txt
- ✅ Check Python version
- ✅ Verify dependency versions
- ✅ Use virtual environment
